text: You can use those cutters to build complex 3D structures similar to some items you would use a CNC or 3D printer instead to build.

author: @AdamBechtol
text: Just adding my voice to the chorus looking forward to a return away from you showing off fancy products ( a dime a dozen on Youtube), back to the DIY creative spirit that made you shine.

replies.author: @AdamBechtol
replies.text: Unless you're on to something. Sure I'd like a 3D printer/cutter, but I couldn't even bring myself to watch this whole video.

text: At £4k+ for a basic unit at 55Watt unit, XTool! cut cost by 50% and stop being so greedy.

author: @AndreaDingbatt
text:
❤ Ohh, I so Need one of These!!
Thank you Robert,,,, for demystifying this kind of thing!!  
You are The Best!!❤

author: @markoneil8286
text: To be honest Robert I miss the days of you in the workshop building things with tools. Not Lazer cutter's and 3d printers. Cos most people still don't have the above devices that would make their prototypes or projects easy to build. That's my comment. Thanks anyway Robert

replies.author: @AdamBechtol
replies.text: Aye

replies.author: @theimpatientbrewer
replies.text: I was just thinking the same thing, then I saw your comment.

author: @AlexanderStewart-k2v
text: I heard that these things can also be used to make graphene?

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Good review. Looking forward to laser cut turbine blades.  Just wondering if there's an opportunity for synergy: might an installed, good value fume extraction system also suffice to properly install and safely operate your wood stove?

author: @BradKarthauser
text: Keep those digits out of the laser's path. We don't want a how-to on 3-D printing new ones. As always, a fascinating video!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - sound advice mate lol

author: @silverpc4611
text: Maybe a CNC router too.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe - it really depends on the individual to be honest

author: @preciousplasticph
text: can you try lasering some sugar and baking soda mixed together to see if you get graphene?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's aninteresting idea - i have done videos on lazing to create graphene before you know

replies.author: @preciousplasticph
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I have seen every video.  I asked Dr Tour about the process of using the sugar and baking soda, and he told me that he has not heard of anyone doing it.  I am in the process of purchasing a Raman machine as it has become too expensive to send the samples to Malaysia. But it is still some time away from its arrival.  I have a automated flash graphene machine here.   We are using anthracite coal doped with carbon black as the feed stock.  However production is very slow.  around 50 grams per hour, assuming the feed hopper does not get jammed.

author: @Mosfet510
text: I think this is a lot of bang for the buck. It could pay for itself in convenience or even a small business. Good one 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate - i have already used it the cuts are beautiful

author: @RiverMersey
text: Hi Rob, with this being video number 22884, have I suddenly missed about 20000 of your videos?!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - thanks mate i will correct that lol

replies.author: @RiverMersey
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering lol, it was funny seeing such a big rise in your already huge catalogue of impressive videos! 

Many thanks for all your hard work, Rob🥰

author: @DudleySmith-ht7nr
text: Excellent discussion, as always. I agree these tools will open "doors" for the innovation of numerous concepts. The only issue is price. Not really a hobby tool. I definitely want one, but I would need to develop something that could be sold to help pay for the device.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: this is without a doubt the higher end of the price scale mate

author: @spehropefhany
text: Thanks, RMS. The closed cooling system is nice- self-contained- unlike the ones that need an external bucket (or chiller). Looks like they're going for a Bambu lab look and feel (ha, you just said exactly that).

replies.text: it is a slick machine

author: @Wez_wolf
text: Thanks for showing this, Ive started on a 5w diode laser, can't cut much but having fun with engraving. I'd love to get a co2 one day. Really appreciate your enthusiastic way to spread awareness of these tools and their real world applications

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @chrisstevens2
text: Robert, since you're more familiar with kilns and metalcasting, I was wondering whether you could make a video on how we could create models in plastic using a 3D printer, and then potentially putting them into a mold to have the plastic be replaced by the metal being poured, as a cheaper alternative to a metal 3D printer?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a good idea mate - I was thinking of upgrading my backyard foundry

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: @chrisstevens2 essentially it's the lost wax casting process, except using plastic

author: @nate_river_
text: A sweet looking piece of kit. Not something you can buy with pocket change, but if you're smart about what you're making, it could easily pay for itself.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree - loads of people seem to be doing very well with this machine

author: @Charlie-Oooooo
text: Hey Robert!  Great to see laser cutting coming more to home-based DIY folks, and excited for you to share your journey with the community!  This company seems to have addressed the safety issues quite well.  Maybe someday soon DIY metal 3D printing will also benefit similarly?  For example, if safety is addressed like this, then use/sales increases, then costs come down for items like the lasers and fine metal powders for sintering.  Exciting times!  I'm sure this is already on your long-term wish/ToDo list!  Cheers mate!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - i am an open book! you are dead right mate - it is right up there lol

author: @settlece
text: Wow I haven't seen you for a long time. Have you had some face work done? You're looking a lot younger.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: wow - seriosly wow - that's cool - no work done - maybe just more sleep lol

author: @Metameglodon
text: I’m not sure if you understand the profound positive effect you’re going to leave on this earth

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @RiverMersey
replies.text: @Metameglodon yes, I fully agree with you - Rob is a genius! 🥰

author: @adamcrofts58
text: not all of us have the advantage of getting equipment for free, from tinkering to hi tech.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I understand I am lucky mate and appreciate the position I am in - do you know what I do with this stuff after I have finished with it? I usually just give it away to people who need it or want it - often to schools

replies.author: @adamcrofts58
replies.text: Thankyou. It is just sometimes that you see people on youtube saying, look you can make this simple machine then proceed to use high end equipment to do it. I still enjoy your shows and you have taught me a lot. keep up the good work,@ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @AdamBechtol
replies.text: Indeed.

author: @ShafaqIftikhar-pw9ld
text: Thankyou robert for a great insight as always ❤

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @grendel1960a
text: I had similar thoughts when I purchased a CNC router, all bar the cutting medium is the same technology- in fact I could buy a laser head for my CNC router if I wished, but I fully agree these machines are the home workshop of the future. and that is a workshop that can be fit into a much smaller footprint (as long as you dont have too many of each machine.)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - too many is indeed a problem but maybe a problem we would all wish to have - i joke mate I am spot on in agreement with you CNC router and Laser cutters are pretty much identical machines

author: @_spartan11796
text: Cool new toy!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure!

author: @jamesross1003
text: Looking forward to a video on performance and a project for which you use it Robert.  Thanks for the video.  My end case use for a laser cutter is probably more suited to a diode laser cutter such as making capacitors  and electronic components burned directly onto a surface, but I have no experience with CO2 lasers so who knows it might work as I would need it to work.  I guess I will have to live vicariously through you Robert while you experiment with the Xtool.  Thanks again!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: The Xtool is a nice machine mate - but diode based cutters have also come a long way - working on a project video right now as it happens!

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering That's great!  Looking forward to it.

author: @johnwynne-qx6br
text: Looks like a great tool/machine. I always fancied getting a milling or laser cnc machine but people had said you need to get into the big ones for them to be any good and after watching quite a few videos could see that they were right. This one looks like a reasonable size and price. Will be interesting to see how it works out. I'm sure it will help a lot with prototyping and projects 😊 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am not sure it is right that you have to get a big one mate - i think it really depends on your use case to be honest

replies.author: @johnwynne-qx6br
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  yes true it was more about the usefulness to what I was hoping to achieve with it 👍
